skill_name: Decode words by identifying syllable patterns
full_description: Decode increasingly difficult multisyllable words by identifying syllable patterns (e.g., transportation, underline, victory, treaty, attachable)

fields.Content-Area Vocabulary: syllable, syllable patterns, chunking, vowel sounds, consonants

fields.ELL Support: Review the concept of syllables and teach common patterns to help all students decode longer words.
fields.Linguistic Competencies: understanding syllable structure and morphology to decode words
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 2 - Use knowledge of regularly spelled syllable patterns to decode multisyllable grade-level words (e.g., read a word such as even by picking the correct syllable breaks)
fields.Short Skill Name: Decode words by identifying syllable patterns

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: recognizing by sight and reading automatically grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words

skill_name: Decode words with common Latin suffixes
full_description: Decode words with common Latin suffixes, such as the -or in elevator or -ment in government, and identify the meanings of the words

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: incorporating knowledge of increasingly difficult affixes in decoding words
fields.Domain Level Expectations: Students begin to demonstrate understanding of age-appropriate vocabulary, including functional and organizational language, applying it in both academic and everyday settings.Students explore word relationships and use a range of strategies to determine the meaning of unknown words as they build and apply core vocabulary in conversing, reading, and responding to texts.Students use several strategies to develop greater understanding of word meanings and relationships, including categorizing and applying knowledge of word roots and affixes, to practice using core vocabulary in academic and daily life.Students apply multiple strategies when encountering unknown words, including using word morphology and beginning dictionaries, in order to increase their core vocabulary, understand word relationships, and strengthen real-life connections to words.Students apply strategies to determine the meanings of words, including nonliteral meaning, in grade-level texts to expand their academic and content-area vocabulary.Students continue to use context, reference materials, and other strategies to determine word meanings and demonstrate understanding of figurative language in order to precisely select words that express actions, emotions, and states of being.Students begin to describe the feelings or images derived from words with similar dictionary definitions and continue to acquire new vocabulary in course content in order to accurately use appropriate domain-specific words when discussing a variety of subjects.Students' knowledge of vocabulary expands to include analogies and the connotative meanings of words and phrases, and words important to comprehension and expression in order to choose correct, precise language when discussing particular subjects.Students apply strategies with greater automaticity to clarify unfamiliar word meanings, including those of figurative language and connotation, in order to use vocabulary correctly and better comprehend literary and informational texts.Students intuitively use multiple strategies to determine word meanings, acquiring knowledge of increasingly sophisticated word relationships and figurative language in order to choose appropriate language and gain deeper comprehension of texts.Students apply multiple strategies to verify word meanings, including the discernment of connotative or nuanced differences, in order to gain independence in use of vocabulary for reading, discussing, and writing about complex texts.Students independently apply multiple strategies to verify word meanings and to analyze sophisticated word relationships in order to expand their understanding and use of vocabulary needed for reading and communicating about complex texts.Students strategize and expand their knowledge of vocabulary by focusing on words that are key to comprehension of complex texts, authors' word choices, and figurative language; they use this knowledge in the precise application of language when reading, writing, speaking, and listening.Students automatically apply multiple strategies flexibly and independently, using a sophisticated vocabulary and understanding of language for reading, discussing, and communicating about college-level texts.

skill_name: Identify purpose for reading and comprehend text
full_description: Identify purpose for reading (e.g., for enjoyment, to answer a question, to learn about a subject) and comprehend on-level texts demonstrated by talking about interesting characters or surprising events, writing an answer to the question, or summarizing what was learned
standard: CP.R.3.FO.FR.3 - Comprehension and purpose for reading

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: understanding the appropriate purpose required for reading, comprehending, and responding to grade- appropriate text
fields.Domain Level Expectations: Students begin to identify basic features of print and initiate alphabetic and phonemic awareness skills in order to establish their understanding of foundations of reading.Students extend their understanding of book features and words in print, utilizing a broad range of alphabetic and phonemic awareness skills to begin to accurately decode simple words.Students use established understanding of books and phonemic awareness skills, applying knowledge of simple phonetic patterns and word structure to accurately and rapidly decode an increased number of grade-level words.Students apply their understanding of increasingly complex phonetic patterns and word structures, using strategies to decode two-syllable grade-level words in text.Students expand the scope of their reading; they decode multisyllable words with increased understanding and automaticity, allowing them to identify the purpose for text.Students read a variety of texts, automatically decoding challenging multisyllable words with fluency and appropriate pace and using strategies to clarify comprehension.Students read broadly, fully utilizing their phonetic knowledge, word analysis skills, and fluency strategies to decode challenging multisyllable words in isolation and within a wide variety of texts.

fields.Linguistic Competencies: identifying one's own purpose for reading to select an appropriate book to read; answering questions about the text to show comprehension
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 2 - Identify purpose for reading (e.g., for enjoyment, to answer a question) and comprehend on-level texts demonstrated by rereading favorite parts to self or other audience or by answering pre-reading questions
fields.Short Skill Name: Identify purpose for reading and comprehend text
fields.Standards: CP.R.3.FO.FR.3 - Comprehension and purpose for reading

skill_name: Read regularly and independently
full_description: Read regularly and independently in third-grade-appropriate texts for sustained periods of time, increasing speed, stamina, and comprehension
standard: CP.R.3.LT.CO.4 - Independent reading and reading strategiesCP.R.3.IF.CI.2 - Independent reading and reading strategies

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: know that reading text fluently requires speed, consistent pace, and understanding of what is being read
fields.Domain Level Expectations: With support, students begin to engage with simple literary texts read aloud by asking and answering questions and retelling details using illustrations, establishing their understanding of story elements and gaining new vocabulary.Students listen to stories for key details, allowing them to retell and identify essential story elements and make predictions as a foundation for close reading of text.Students begin to read literary texts independently and make connections to ideas through personal experience and prior knowledge. They use key details to retell stories and describe a stated moral, gaining a firmer sense of the basic elements of stories.Students recount a text's key ideas, details, and central message as a foundation for interpreting literary elements and for reflecting on and drawing simple conclusions about the meaning of stories, fables, and folktales from diverse cultures.Students use strategies such as rereading, predicting, and background knowledge to comprehend texts. They interpret literary elements and important details in texts to answer their questions and deepen understanding of the meaning of literary texts from around the world.Students begin to summarize stories and rely on context clues to understand literary texts. They use close reading and textual evidence when making inferences in order to better understand literary elements and their contribution to texts from different genres.Students apply increasingly sophisticated reading strategies to comprehend texts, accurately drawing from textual evidence. They compare and contrast how literary elements are used to create more complex themes as a foundation for deeper literary analysis in later grades. Students continue to use various strategies to understand literary texts across genres, while analyzing connections among story elements, summarizing and drawing conclusions that deepen their knowledge of literature.Students read widely and automatically correct their understanding of a text. They analyze the influence of literary elements with increased sophistication, drawing conclusions and interpreting complex literary texts in order to develop summaries that accurately reflect the text's overall meaning.Students make genuine connections to literary texts, and they deepen their understanding of how related narrative elements and themes develop, weighing evidence in order to make a comprehensive interpretation of texts.Students draw larger conclusions from literary texts, connecting passages to the overall meaning and analyzing relationships between complex textual elements and ways they contribute to significant ideas and themes.Students implement more advanced reading strategies, analyzing how literary elements shape the narrative and can refine the meaning and significance of literary text. They infer themes and draw conclusions based on a broad spectrum of increasingly sophisticated textual evidence.Students readily apply close reading strategies to analyze the interaction of narrative elements and their contribution to a literary text's deeper meaning. Making inferences and considering multiple interpretations and cultural contexts in a wide array of genres, they prepare to synthesize ideas in more complex texts in Grade 12.Students automatically utilize a full array of strategies when approaching challenging literary texts, synthesizing complex concepts in order to interpret ambiguous themes. They infer meaning while considering multiple interpretations of text, including cutural and intellectual influences.
fields.ELL Support: Model fluent reading and then have a collaborative group read aloud a simple sentence or two.  Have students read independently as much as possible in their Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD).
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 2 - Read regularly and independently in second-grade-appropriate texts for sustained periods of time, increasing speed, stamina, and comprehensionGrade 2 - Read on-level texts aloud with increasing smoothness and appropriate expression (e.g., using different voices for different characters)

fields.Domain Level Expectations: Students begin to demonstrate understanding of age-appropriate vocabulary, including functional and organizational language, applying it in both academic and everyday settings.Students explore word relationships and use a range of strategies to determine the meaning of unknown words as they build and apply core vocabulary in conversing, reading, and responding to texts.Students use several strategies to develop greater understanding of word meanings and relationships, including categorizing and applying knowledge of word roots and affixes, to practice using core vocabulary in academic and daily life.Students apply multiple strategies when encountering unknown words, including using word morphology and beginning dictionaries, in order to increase their core vocabulary, understand word relationships, and strengthen real-life connections to words.Students apply strategies to determine the meanings of words, including nonliteral meaning, in grade-level texts to expand their academic and content-area vocabulary.Students continue to use context, reference materials, and other strategies to determine word meanings and demonstrate understanding of figurative language in order to precisely select words that express actions, emotions, and states of being.Students begin to describe the feelings or images derived from words with similar dictionary definitions and continue to acquire new vocabulary in course content in order to accurately use appropriate domain-specific words when discussing a variety of subjects.Students' knowledge of vocabulary expands to include analogies and the connotative meanings of words and phrases, and words important to comprehension and expression in order to choose correct, precise language when discussing particular subjects.Students apply strategies with greater automaticity to clarify unfamiliar word meanings, including those of figurative language and connotation, in order to use vocabulary correctly and better comprehend literary and informational texts.Students intuitively use multiple strategies to determine word meanings, acquiring knowledge of increasingly sophisticated word relationships and figurative language in order to choose appropriate language and gain deeper comprehension of texts.Students apply multiple strategies to verify word meanings, including the discernment of connotative or nuanced differences, in order to gain independence in use of vocabulary for reading, discussing, and writing about complex texts.Students independently apply multiple strategies to verify word meanings and to analyze sophisticated word relationships in order to expand their understanding and use of vocabulary needed for reading and communicating about complex texts.Students strategize and expand their knowledge of vocabulary by focusing on words that are key to comprehension of complex texts, authors' word choices, and figurative language; they use this knowledge in the precise application of language when reading, writing, speaking, and listening.Students automatically apply multiple strategies flexibly and independently, using a sophisticated vocabulary and understanding of language for reading, discussing, and communicating about college-level texts.
fields.Short Skill Name: Explain shades of meaning between synonyms

skill_name: Refer to informational text to answer questions
full_description: Ask and answer questions about an informational text and refer to the text to support answers

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: understanding key details related to a topic; determining where in the text particular information can be found
fields.Domain Level Expectations: The foundations for developing close-reading skills begin when students listen for key details and engage with informational text read aloud.With support, students build on foundations as they begin to read and engage with text, applying basic reading strategies, asking and answering questions, making connections and simple comparisons, and identifying a topic, key ideas, and information.As students continue to listen to and begin to read texts on their own, they develop an understanding of the main points and key details as well as how to apply reading strategies.As students continue to build fluency and begin to read informational texts to gain content knowledge, they draw simple conclusions, identify main ideas and key details, describe relationships between ideas (cause/effect, order of events), and distinguish fact from opinion. As students begin to read more diverse texts to expand their knowledge of different subjects, they identify main ideas, explaining how textual details support them. They identify opinions, describe cause-and-effect relationships, make predictions, and draw simple conclusions, using evidence and illustrations from the text.As students engage in domain-specific learning in multiple subjects, they gain information through close reading, examining main ideas and supporting details. They explain the text's meaning and support their explanations and inferences with textual details and examples.Students continue to gain greater understanding of historical, scientific, and technical texts by grasping key ideas and details and making inferences, citing accurate information from the text.Students deepen understanding of subjects by reading a variety of informational texts, applying independent reading strategies, and integrating types of information. They determine how ideas and information are introduced and developed, citing evidence to support analysis and conclusions. As students encounter more complex domain-specific informational texts, they use organizational patterns as guides for comprehension. They analyze the development of central ideas and cite textual evidence to support analysis.As students encounter increasingly complex informational texts and technical processes, they are able to automatically monitor their comprehension as well as synthesize information, explain development of central ideas, and draw conclusions, weighing evidence to support their analysis.Students draw larger conclusions about informational texts based on their analysis and support their conclusions with strong, thorough evidence. They analyze the author's development of the central ideas and understand complex technical or scientific processes, applying reading strategies with automaticity.To understand the implications of complex informational texts, students analyze how the central idea and author's analysis emerge and are shaped and refined, drawing larger conclusions about the text's meaning or significance, citing strong and thorough evidence, and evaluating conflicting information.Students read a wide variety of sophisticated informational and technical texts. They analyze the interaction and development of central ideas and sequences of events. Students support conclusions with thorough evidence, noting where texts leave matters uncertain.Students display a full range of analytical tools which they apply to many challenging informational texts, synthesizing a text's ideas to create complex, comprehensive, well-supported analyses that evaluate the development of the author's thesis, reveal ambiguities, and explain interactions among ideas.
fields.ELL Support: Clarify key details from a text by paraphrasing with more simplistic language.
fields.Linguistic Competencies: using complete sentences to ask and answer questions
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 2 - Ask and answer who, what, where, when, why, and how questions about key details in an informational textGrade 2 - Locate key details in an informational text and determine what they describe or explainGrade 2 - Draw simple conclusions about an informational text using evidence and details from text and illustrations
fields.Short Skill Name: Refer to informational text to answer questions

skill_name: Interpret visually presented information
full_description: Interpret common types of visually presented information (e.g., maps, photographs, graphs, information tables)

fields.Domain Level Expectations: With support, students begin to understand the different purposes for reading informational texts of various topics, identifying the texts’ authors and illustrators as a foundation for early reading comprehension skills.To develop reading comprehension, students become familiar with a range of informational texts, identifying the roles of both author and illustrator and identifying the author's purpose for writing, while also using text features such as titles and illustrations to make predictions.Students discern general differences in printed and digital informational material, noting the role of various text features and closely examining the meaning and effects of word choices to develop greater comprehension of the author's viewpoints and purpose.To engage more fully in their reading, students further explore the purposes and features of informational texts and media as they examine sequential order, figurative language, and how authors use language to create an effect for readers.As students develop the ability to seek information from informational texts, they interpret the author’s purpose and use of sensory language, sequential events and steps, visually presented information, and the overall structure and characteristics of nonfictional genres.To further develop comprehension of informational texts, students continue to analyze the structure, visual presentation of text features, and author’s purpose in order to correctly interpret what is read.Students identify and analyze common structures and language used in informational texts and media, determining the author’s purpose and connections between ideas and making comparisons between digital and printed texts, enabling them to further develop their overall reading comprehension and engagement.To engage fully with nonfiction, students’ analysis of informational text expands to include making comparisons between the various forms (argument pieces, autobiographies) and examining relationships between organizational structure and the author or between the larger ideas of a text.To establish a framework for understanding different works of nonfiction and authors' perspectives, students continue to analyze the characteristics and devices used, such as sequence, graphical components, and the effects of language.Students understand in greater depth how to use informational texts as they continue to analyze how language and other techniques establish an author’s style and voice, and they analyze how presentation of information affects the overall message and impact of a text or media message.To navigate and make full use of informational works, including those with varying accounts of the same subject, students analyze more complex texts and media with a focus on the interrelationship of an author’s background, word choice, and message.As they make more sophisticated interpretations of a variety of nonfictional works, students analyze in greater depth how authors impact an audience with background, language, refinement of ideas and processes, and supporting evidence.To make more complex interpretations of nonfictional works and media, students analyze the impact of structure, genre characteristics, text features, and authors’ language choices on a text and how the author’s intended audience is influenced.Students intuitively evaluate the effectiveness of structure, text features, cultural and historical influence, and an author’s use of language and literary devices on informational texts.

skill_name: Make / check predictions about texts
full_description: Make predictions about texts based on chapter titles, headings, tables of contents, story patterns, and relationships between events and ideas, and explain whether these predictions are correct
standard: CP.R.3.LT.CO.3 - Simple conclusions and textual supportCP.R.3.IF.CI.2 - Independent reading and reading strategies

fields.Domain Level Expectations: With support, students begin to engage with simple literary texts read aloud by asking and answering questions and retelling details using illustrations, establishing their understanding of story elements and gaining new vocabulary.Students listen to stories for key details, allowing them to retell and identify essential story elements and make predictions as a foundation for close reading of text.Students begin to read literary texts independently and make connections to ideas through personal experience and prior knowledge. They use key details to retell stories and describe a stated moral, gaining a firmer sense of the basic elements of stories.Students recount a text's key ideas, details, and central message as a foundation for interpreting literary elements and for reflecting on and drawing simple conclusions about the meaning of stories, fables, and folktales from diverse cultures.Students use strategies such as rereading, predicting, and background knowledge to comprehend texts. They interpret literary elements and important details in texts to answer their questions and deepen understanding of the meaning of literary texts from around the world.Students begin to summarize stories and rely on context clues to understand literary texts. They use close reading and textual evidence when making inferences in order to better understand literary elements and their contribution to texts from different genres.Students apply increasingly sophisticated reading strategies to comprehend texts, accurately drawing from textual evidence. They compare and contrast how literary elements are used to create more complex themes as a foundation for deeper literary analysis in later grades. Students continue to use various strategies to understand literary texts across genres, while analyzing connections among story elements, summarizing and drawing conclusions that deepen their knowledge of literature.Students read widely and automatically correct their understanding of a text. They analyze the influence of literary elements with increased sophistication, drawing conclusions and interpreting complex literary texts in order to develop summaries that accurately reflect the text's overall meaning.Students make genuine connections to literary texts, and they deepen their understanding of how related narrative elements and themes develop, weighing evidence in order to make a comprehensive interpretation of texts.Students draw larger conclusions from literary texts, connecting passages to the overall meaning and analyzing relationships between complex textual elements and ways they contribute to significant ideas and themes.Students implement more advanced reading strategies, analyzing how literary elements shape the narrative and can refine the meaning and significance of literary text. They infer themes and draw conclusions based on a broad spectrum of increasingly sophisticated textual evidence.Students readily apply close reading strategies to analyze the interaction of narrative elements and their contribution to a literary text's deeper meaning. Making inferences and considering multiple interpretations and cultural contexts in a wide array of genres, they prepare to synthesize ideas in more complex texts in Grade 12.Students automatically utilize a full array of strategies when approaching challenging literary texts, synthesizing complex concepts in order to interpret ambiguous themes. They infer meaning while considering multiple interpretations of text, including cutural and intellectual influences.

fields.Domain Level Expectations: The foundations for developing close-reading skills begin when students listen for key details and engage with informational text read aloud.With support, students build on foundations as they begin to read and engage with text, applying basic reading strategies, asking and answering questions, making connections and simple comparisons, and identifying a topic, key ideas, and information.As students continue to listen to and begin to read texts on their own, they develop an understanding of the main points and key details as well as how to apply reading strategies.As students continue to build fluency and begin to read informational texts to gain content knowledge, they draw simple conclusions, identify main ideas and key details, describe relationships between ideas (cause/effect, order of events), and distinguish fact from opinion. As students begin to read more diverse texts to expand their knowledge of different subjects, they identify main ideas, explaining how textual details support them. They identify opinions, describe cause-and-effect relationships, make predictions, and draw simple conclusions, using evidence and illustrations from the text.As students engage in domain-specific learning in multiple subjects, they gain information through close reading, examining main ideas and supporting details. They determine how ideas and information are introduced and developed, citing evidence to support analysis and conclusions. As students encounter more complex domain-specific informational texts, they use organizational patterns as guides for comprehension. They analyze the development of central ideas and cite textual evidence to support analysis.As students encounter increasingly complex informational texts and technical processes, they are able to automatically monitor their comprehension as well as synthesize information, explain development of central ideas, and draw conclusions, weighing evidence to support their analysis.Students draw larger conclusions about informational texts based on their analysis and support their conclusions with strong, thorough evidence. They analyze the author's development of the central ideas and understand complex technical or scientific processes, applying reading strategies with automaticity.To understand the implications of complex informational texts, students analyze how the central idea and author's analysis emerge and are shaped and refined, drawing larger conclusions about the text's meaning or significance, citing strong and thorough evidence, and evaluating conflicting information.Students read a wide variety of sophisticated informational and technical texts. They analyze the interaction and development of central ideas and sequences of events. Students support conclusions with thorough evidence, noting where texts leave matters uncertain.Students display a full range of analytical tools which they apply to many challenging informational texts, synthesizing a text's ideas to create complex, comprehensive, well-supported analyses that evaluate the development of the author's thesis, reveal ambiguities, and explain interactions among ideas.

full_description: Understand that texts can communicate a feeling or mood

fields.Domain Level Expectations: As a foundation for early reading comprehension, with support, students begin to recognize common types and formats of text, understanding that a text has an author and illustrator, and a beginning and an end.Through early exposure to different genres, students become aware of sensory language and sound devices, and develop an understanding of simple text features and structures.By exploring texts, students begin to recognize the general structural differences between fiction and nonfiction. They are exposed to sound devices, and playful and figurative language to develop basic understanding of how word choice affects text.Students identify patterns and imagery as they build vocabulary, understanding both literal and figurative language. They describe the differences between genres and explore basic text structures, becoming more aware of how these elements influence text.  Students understand that words can create images and communicate a mood or tone. They describe genre characteristics and recognize parts of books, plays, and poems to help determine common forms and organization of text. By broadening their knowledge of author's word choice, structural elements, and specific terms for literary forms, students are able to comprehend and discuss characteristics of a variety of texts, including origin myths and traditional literature.Students determine the meaning of figurative language and analyze the contributions of sensory detail and literary devices. To further develop reading comprehension, they compare structures and identify terminology for the parts of different literary texts.In order to understand the effects of author's word choice, students determine connotative meaning and examine figurative language, sensory detail, and common literary devices. They expand their knowledge of different forms of literary text, including traditional stories, by analyzing common elements and organization.As students deepen their understanding of the author's craft and the underlying meaning of a text, they analyze how language choices, structure and form, and common archetypes and allusions all affect the meaning and impact of a literary work.Students analyze an author's use of language, structural choices, and genre characteristics in order to understand their impact on meaning and contributions to the text's development and style.  Continuing to analyze word choice and structure through exposure to a variety of genres, students deepen their knowledge of the effects of these elements on text meaning, mood, and tone in order to expand their comprehension of increasingly sophisticated texts. To interpret more complex works, students continue to analyze genre characteristics and structural choices, and their consideration of craft and structure broadens to include the cumulative impact of an author's word choice on a text. Students apply increased understanding of the aesthetic impact of author's language choices and sophisticated structural choices as they analyze and evaluate the overall meaning of more complicated and diverse texts.To develop skills of literary criticism, students examine the aesthetic impact of word choices and literary techniques, analyzing and comparing how genre characteristics, structural choices, and devices such as irony are used to create the meaning and tone in a broad array of complex literary works.

digital informational material, noting the role of various text features and closely examining the meaning and effects of word choices to develop greater comprehension of the author's viewpoints and purpose.To engage more fully in their reading, students further explore the purposes and features of informational texts and media as they examine sequential order, figurative language, and how sequential events and steps, visually presented information, and the overall structure and characteristics of nonfictional genres.To further develop comprehension of informational texts, students overall reading comprehension and engagement.To engage fully with nonfiction, students’ analysis of informational text expands to include making comparisons between the various forms (argument pieces, autobiographies) and examining relationships between organizational structure and the author or between the larger ideas of a text.To establish a framework for understanding different works of nonfiction and authors' perspectives, students continue to analyze the characteristics and devices used, such as sequence, graphical components, and the effects of language.Students understand in greater depth how to use informational texts as they continue to analyze how language and other techniques establish an author’s style and voice, and they analyze how presentation of information affects the overall message and impact of a text or media message.To navigate and make full use of informational works, including those with varying accounts of the same subject, students analyze more complex texts and media with a focus on the interrelationship of an author’s background, word choice, and message.As they make more sophisticated interpretations of a variety of nonfictional works, students analyze in greater depth how authors impact an audience with background, language, refinement of ideas and processes, and supporting evidence.To make more complex interpretations of nonfictional works and media, students analyze the impact of structure, genre characteristics, text features, and authors’ language choices on a text and how the author’s intended audience is influenced.Students intuitively evaluate the effectiveness of structure, text features, cultural and historical influence, and an author’s use of language and literary devices on informational texts.

skill_name: Tell how illustrations contribute to the story
full_description: Explain how a text's illustrations reflect and contribute to the story (e.g., describe actions, create mood, emphasize aspects of a character or setting)

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: recognizing how illustrations relate to the story; recognizing details in the illustrations that contribute to the story
fields.Domain Level Expectations: Students find similarities between different versions (audio, video, live version) of a printed text, and with support, make comparisons between characters and events in stories as a foundation for determining differences in literary texts independently. Students draw from both words and illustrations to describe literary elements and to make comparisons within and between texts, in order to gain deeper understanding of what is read.Students continue to draw from both the words and illustrations of print or digital texts, including similar versions of a story, for greater understanding and the ability to make personal connections and comparisons between texts across cultures.Students compare traditional tales to deepen understanding of literary elements and common themes, and understand how illustrations contribute to a story as a foundation for interpreting visual and multimedia elements in later grades.Students interpret an oral or visual presentation of a text to confirm their understanding, and they make inferences and relevant comparisons between works of traditional literature across cultures in order to understand how similar themes are used in different texts.Students explore the contributions of visual and multimedia elements to a text; they make comparisons between stories in similar genres to deepen understading and in preparation for comparing different perspectives in literary texts to their own.Students make comparisons between written texts and live or multimedia versions, exploring how literature in various forms is perceived, and they continue to make connections between texts and personal experience and compare genres to deepen understanding of similar literary themes.Students analyze how the techniques used in each literary medium (film, live, recorded, multimedia) affect the piece, and they make comparisons between fictional and nonfictional accounts of a historical period to deepen their understanding of literature.Students analyze the extent to which techniques used in each literary medium (film, live, recorded, multimedia) affect the piece; they compare modern works of fiction with similarly themed traditional stories in order to determine how the texts stay true to source material.Students apply content-specific knowledge (language arts, history, social studies) to make critical comparisons between texts; they analyze how a specific subject or scene is treated in two different artistic mediums, as well as how modern works draw from traditional source material in order to gain greater understanding of the meaning of various story treatments.Students analyze with greater sophistication how a specific subject or scene is treated in different artistic mediums, as well as how authors draw from traditional, influential source material in modern works in order to gain greater insight into the meaning of literary texts.Students analyze multiple versions of increasingly complex literary texts to evaluate how each version interprets the source material; they compare ideas and themes in texts in order to understand how literary works reflect the time period in which they were written.Students critique complex literary works, considering genre and author's intent or philosophy as well as literary elements, structure, and levels of theme and meaning. skill_name: Recount fables and myths from diverse cultures
full_description: Recount stories such as fables, folktales, and myths from diverse cultures, including key details and important events
standard: CP.R.3.LT.CO.1 - Comprehension of events and details

fields.Domain Level Expectations: With support, students begin to engage with simple literary texts read aloud by asking and answering questions and retelling details using illustrations, establishing their understanding of story elements and gaining new vocabulary.Students listen to stories for key details, allowing them to retell and identify essential story elements and make predictions as a foundation for close reading of text.Students begin to read literary texts independently and make connections to ideas through personal experience and prior knowledge. They use key details to retell stories and describe a stated moral, gaining a firmer sense of the basic elements of stories.Students recount a text's key ideas, details, and central message as a foundation for interpreting literary elements and for reflecting on and drawing simple conclusions about the meaning of stories, fables, and folktales from diverse cultures.Students use strategies such as rereading, predicting, and background knowledge to comprehend texts. They interpret literary elements and important details in texts to answer their questions and deepen understanding of the meaning of literary texts from around the world.Students begin to summarize stories and rely on context clues to understand literary texts. They use close reading and textual evidence when making inferences in order to better understand literary elements and their contribution to texts from different genres.Students apply increasingly sophisticated reading strategies to comprehend texts, accurately drawing from textual evidence. They compare and contrast how literary elements are used to create more complex themes as a foundation for deeper literary analysis in later grades. Students continue to use various strategies to understand literary texts across genres, while analyzing connections among story elements, summarizing and drawing conclusions that deepen their knowledge of literature.Students read widely and automatically correct their understanding of a text. They analyze the influence of literary elements with increased sophistication, drawing conclusions and interpreting complex literary texts in order to develop summaries that accurately reflect the text's overall meaning.Students make genuine connections to literary texts, and they deepen their understanding of how related narrative elements and themes develop, weighing evidence in order to make a comprehensive interpretation of texts.Students draw larger conclusions from literary texts, connecting passages to the overall meaning and analyzing relationships between complex textual elements and ways they contribute to significant ideas and themes.Students implement more advanced reading strategies, analyzing how literary elements shape the narrative and can refine the meaning and significance of literary text. They infer themes and draw conclusions based on a broad spectrum of increasingly sophisticated textual evidence.Students readily apply close reading strategies to analyze the interaction of narrative elements and their contribution to a literary text's deeper meaning. Making inferences and considering multiple interpretations and cultural contexts in a wide array of genres, they prepare to synthesize ideas in more complex texts in Grade 12.Students automatically utilize a full array of strategies when approaching challenging literary texts, synthesizing complex concepts in order to interpret ambiguous themes. They infer meaning while considering multiple interpretations of text, including cutural and intellectual influences.

full_description: Use prior knowledge and evidence from the text to draw simple conclusions about information or events
standard: CP.R.3.IF.CI.2 - Independent reading and reading strategies

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: activating prior knowledge
fields.Domain Level Expectations: The foundations for developing close-reading skills begin when students listen for key details and engage with informational text read aloud.With support, students build on foundations as they begin to read and engage with text, applying basic reading strategies, asking and answering questions, making connections and simple comparisons, and identifying a topic, key ideas, and information.As students continue to listen to and begin to read texts on their own, they develop an understanding of the main points and key details as well as how to apply reading strategies.As students continue to build fluency and begin to read informational texts to gain content knowledge, they draw simple conclusions, identify main ideas and key details, describe relationships between ideas (cause/effect, order of events), and distinguish fact from opinion. As students begin to read more diverse texts to expand their knowledge of different subjects, they identify main ideas, explaining how textual details support them. They identify opinions, describe cause-and-effect relationships, make predictions, and draw simple conclusions, using evidence and illustrations from the text.As students engage in domain-specific learning in multiple subjects, they gain information through close reading, examining main ideas and supporting details. They explain the text's meaning and support their explanations and inferences with textual details and examples.Students continue to gain greater understanding of historical, scientific, and technical texts by grasping key ideas and details and making inferences, citing accurate information from the text.Students deepen understanding of subjects by reading a variety of informational texts, applying independent reading strategies, and integrating types of information. They determine how ideas and information are introduced and developed, citing evidence to support analysis and conclusions. As students encounter more complex domain-specific informational texts, they use organizational patterns as guides for comprehension. They analyze the development of central ideas and cite textual evidence to support analysis.As students encounter increasingly complex informational texts and technical processes, they are able to automatically monitor their comprehension as well as synthesize information, explain development of central ideas, and draw conclusions, weighing evidence to support their analysis.Students draw larger conclusions about informational texts based on their analysis and support their conclusions with strong, thorough evidence. They analyze the author's development of the central ideas and understand complex technical or scientific processes, applying reading strategies with automaticity.To understand the implications of complex informational texts, students analyze how the central idea and author's analysis emerge and are shaped and refined, drawing larger conclusions about the text's meaning or significance, citing strong and thorough evidence, and evaluating conflicting information.Students read a wide variety of sophisticated informational and technical texts. They analyze the interaction and development of central ideas and sequences of events. Students support conclusions with thorough evidence, noting where texts leave matters uncertain.Students display a full range of analytical tools which they apply to many challenging informational texts, synthesizing a text's ideas to create complex, comprehensive, well-supported analyses that evaluate the development of the author's thesis, reveal ambiguities, and explain interactions among ideas.

skill_name: Describe / distinguish common literary genres
full_description: Describe genre characteristics (e.g., structure), distinguishing among common forms of literature (e.g., plays, fiction, nonfiction, rhyming and nonrhyming poetry)

fields.Domain Level Expectations: As a foundation for early reading comprehension, with support, students begin to recognize common types and formats of text, understanding that a text has an author and illustrator, and a beginning and an end.Through early exposure to different genres, students become aware of sensory language and sound devices, and develop an understanding of simple text features and structures.By exploring texts, students begin to recognize the general structural differences between fiction and nonfiction. They are exposed to sound devices, and playful and figurative language to develop basic understanding of how word choice affects text.Students identify patterns and imagery as they build vocabulary, understanding both literal and figurative language. They describe the differences between genres and explore basic text structures, becoming more aware of how these elements influence text.  Students understand that words can create images and communicate a mood or tone. They describe genre characteristics and recognize parts of books, plays, and poems to help determine common forms and organization of text. By broadening their knowledge of author's word choice, structural elements, and specific terms for literary forms, students are able to comprehend and discuss characteristics of a variety of texts, including origin myths and traditional literature.Students determine the meaning of figurative language and analyze the contributions of sensory detail and literary devices. To further develop reading comprehension, they compare structures and identify terminology for the parts of different literary texts.In order to understand the effects of author's word choice, students determine connotative meaning and examine figurative language, sensory detail, and common literary devices. They expand their knowledge of different forms of literary text, including traditional stories, by analyzing common elements and organization.As students deepen their understanding of the author's craft and the underlying meaning of a text, they analyze how language choices, structure and form, and common archetypes and allusions all affect the meaning and impact of a literary work.Students analyze an author's use of language, structural choices, and genre characteristics in order to understand their impact on meaning and contributions to the text's development and style.  Continuing to analyze word choice and structure through exposure to a variety of genres, students deepen their knowledge of the effects of these elements on text meaning, mood, and tone in order to expand their comprehension of increasingly sophisticated texts. To interpret more complex works, students continue to analyze genre characteristics and structural choices, and their consideration of craft and structure broadens to include the cumulative impact of an author's word choice on a text. Students apply increased understanding of the aesthetic impact of author's language choices and sophisticated structural choices as they analyze and evaluate the overall meaning of more complicated and diverse texts.To develop skills of literary criticism, students examine the aesthetic impact of word choices and literary techniques, analyzing and comparing how genre characteristics, structural choices, and devices such as irony are used to create the meaning and tone in a broad array of complex literary works.
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 1 - Explain the general differences between books that provide information and books that tell storiesGrade 2 - Distinguish among genres including poetry, plays, fiction (stories, folktales), and nonfiction (social studies, technical)Grade 2 - Explain the differences between genres including poetry, plays, and fiction (e.g., stories, folktales)
fields.Short Skill Name: Describe / distinguish common literary genres

skill_name: Compare different forms of informational text
full_description: Describe genre characteristics (e.g., structure, content), distinguishing among common forms of informational text (e.g., narrative, descriptive, expository, persuasive)

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: understanding that authors write for different purposes and structure their work accordingly
fields.Content-Area Vocabulary: informational, how-to, persuade, convince, explain, describe, entertain
fields.Domain Level Expectations: With support, students begin to understand the different purposes for reading informational texts of various topics, identifying the texts’ authors and illustrators as a foundation for early reading comprehension skills.To develop reading comprehension, students become familiar with a range of informational texts, identifying the roles of both author and illustrator and identifying the author's purpose for writing, while also using text features such as titles and illustrations to make predictions.Students discern general differences in printed and digital informational material, noting the role of various text features and closely examining the meaning and effects of word choices to develop greater comprehension of the author's viewpoints and purpose.To engage more fully in their reading, students further explore the purposes and features of informational texts and media as they examine sequential order, figurative language, and how authors use language to create an effect for readers.As students develop the ability to seek information from informational texts, they interpret the author’s purpose and use of sensory language, sequential events and steps, visually presented information, and the overall structure and characteristics of nonfictional genres.To further develop comprehension of informational texts, students continue to analyze the structure, visual presentation of text features, and author’s purpose in order to correctly interpret what is read.Students identify and analyze common structures and language used in informational texts and media, determining the author’s purpose and connections between ideas and making comparisons between digital and printed texts, enabling them to further develop their overall reading comprehension and engagement.To engage fully with nonfiction, students’ analysis of informational text expands to include making comparisons between the various forms (argument pieces, autobiographies) and examining relationships between organizational structure and the author or between the larger ideas of a text.To establish a framework for understanding different works of nonfiction and authors' perspectives, students continue to analyze the characteristics and devices used, such as sequence, graphical components, and the effects of language.Students understand in greater depth how to use informational texts as they continue to analyze how language and other techniques establish an author’s style and voice, and they analyze how presentation of information affects the overall message and impact of a text or media message.To navigate and make full use of informational works, including those with varying accounts of the same subject, students analyze more complex texts and media with a focus on the interrelationship of an author’s background, word choice, and message.As they make more sophisticated interpretations of a variety of nonfictional works, students analyze in greater depth how authors impact an audience with background, language, refinement of ideas and processes, and supporting evidence.To make more complex interpretations of nonfictional works and media, students analyze the impact of structure, genre characteristics, text features, and authors’ language choices on a text and how the author’s intended audience is influenced.Students intuitively evaluate the effectiveness of structure, text features, cultural and historical influence, and an author’s use of language and literary devices on informational texts.
fields.ELL Support: Model using story webs to record information about the structure and content of various informational texts and model using Venn diagrams to compare differences recorded in the webs.
fields.Linguistic Competencies: using specific language in more complex sentences; using content-area vocabulary
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 1 - Explain the general differences between books that provide information and books that tell storiesGrade 2 - Explain the differences between genres of informational texts (e.g., biographies, content-area books, directions)Grade 2 - Explain why an author wrote an informational text (e.g., to tell about sharks, to tell how to cook spaghetti)
fields.Short Skill Name: Compare different forms of informational text

skill_name: Explain how key details support the main idea
full_description: Describe the key details of an informational text and explain how they support the main idea

fields.Conceptual Knowledge: understanding the main idea of a text and recognizing the key details that support it
fields.Domain Level Expectations: The foundations for developing close-reading skills begin when students listen for key details and engage with informational text read aloud.With support, students build on foundations as they begin to read and engage with text, applying basic reading strategies, asking and answering questions, making connections and simple comparisons, and identifying a topic, key ideas, and information.As students continue to listen to and begin to read texts on their own, they develop an understanding of the main points and key details as well as how to apply reading strategies.As students continue to build fluency and begin to read informational texts to gain content knowledge, they draw simple conclusions, identify main ideas and key details, describe relationships between ideas (cause/effect, order of events), and distinguish fact from opinion. As students begin to read more diverse texts to expand their knowledge of different subjects, they identify main ideas, explaining how textual details support them. They identify opinions, describe cause-and-effect relationships, make predictions, and draw simple conclusions, using evidence and illustrations from the text.As students engage in domain-specific learning in multiple subjects, they gain information through close reading, examining main ideas and supporting details. They explain the text's meaning and support their explanations and inferences with textual details and examples.Students continue to gain greater understanding of historical, scientific, and technical texts by grasping key ideas and details and making inferences, citing accurate information from the text.Students deepen understanding of subjects by reading a variety of informational texts, applying independent reading strategies, and integrating types of information. They determine how ideas and information are introduced and developed, citing evidence to support analysis and conclusions. As students encounter more complex domain-specific informational texts, they use organizational patterns as guides for comprehension. They analyze the development of central ideas and cite textual evidence to support analysis.As students encounter increasingly complex informational texts and technical processes, they are able to automatically monitor their comprehension as well as synthesize information, explain development of central ideas, and draw conclusions, weighing evidence to support their analysis.Students draw larger conclusions about informational texts based on their analysis and support their conclusions with strong, thorough evidence. They analyze the author's development of the central ideas and understand complex technical or scientific processes, applying reading strategies with automaticity.To understand the implications of complex informational texts, students analyze how the central idea and author's analysis emerge and are shaped and refined, drawing larger conclusions about the text's meaning or significance, citing strong and thorough evidence, and evaluating conflicting information.Students read a wide variety of sophisticated informational and technical texts. They analyze the interaction and development of central ideas and sequences of events. Students support conclusions with thorough evidence, noting where texts leave matters uncertain.Students display a full range of analytical tools which they apply to many challenging informational texts, synthesizing a text's ideas to create complex, comprehensive, well-supported analyses that evaluate the development of the author's thesis, reveal ambiguities, and explain interactions among ideas.
fields.ELL Support: Use graphic organizers to identify main concepts and supporting details.
fields.Linguistic Competencies: using compound sentences that require simple conjunctions
fields.Prerequisite Skills: Grade 2 - Ask and answer who, what, where, when, why, and how questions about key details in an informational textGrade 2 - Locate key details in an informational text and determine what they describe or explainGrade 2 - Explain how specific images contribute to and clarify the information in a text (e.g., how an image of the earth next to the sun adds to what the text says about the relative sizes of the earth and the sun)Grade 2 - Identify what the author wants to describe or explain, or what question the author is answering in an informational textGrade 2 - Describe how the reasons an author gives support the points the author makes in a text

skill_name: Use evidence to connect texts with similar ideas
full_description: Make connections (e.g., thematic links, author analysis) between literary and informational texts with similar ideas and provide textual evidence

fields.Domain Level Expectations: Students seek information reflected in different approaches and mediums, using illustrations to understand specific ideas from the text and developing a greater comprehension of the author's points and reasoning.Students engage more fully with informational texts, relating to personal and community experiences and using information extracted from graphics along with ideas from the text and comparing the main ideas of different texts covering the same topic.As students seek content-specific information from different texts, they compare the ideas and details in them by separating each text into the author's claims, evidence, and the logic connecting them.Students thoughtfully draw on multiple perspectives by interpreting and comparing various texts on a topic and integrating information from multiple sources and presentations.When learning about a topic, students select sources and integrate information, and when analyzing media and argument, students connect claims to supporting evidence, understand what makes evidence credible, and identify persuasive strategies.Students interpret and integrate information from a wide range of texts, and they assess evidence and claims, determining whether arguments are convincing, while also considering author's perspective, persuasive techniques, and audience.Deepening their understanding of how authors' perspectives on a topic differ, students connect and assess an argument's logic and evidence, comparing purposes and areas of emphasis and analyzing how an argument is impacted by the medium in which it is presented.Students broaden their analysis of arguments in text to include identifying and explaining counterarguments, bias, false statements, and fallacious reasoning, synthesizing information in order to draw accurate conclusions.Students further analyze the logic and reasoning of arguments presented in a range of historically significant texts, including responses to opposing views and identifying fallacies and use of techniques, such as propoganda, to further the author's purpose.Students evaluate the strengths and weaknesses of evidence presented in an argument, determining the impact of rhetorical devices, bias, and logical fallacies to further an author's purpose and apply this analysis across mediums to synthesize understanding of a range of perspectives in text.Students use a critical lens to interpret texts and synthesize information from different sources and presentations. They evaluate an argument's quality, analyzing use of evidence and support, rhetorical devices, and fallacies, and they analyze media's influence.Students compare the development, purpose, and style of complex texts and evaluate complex arguments and media messages, dismantling faulty logic, uncovering the author's values and unstated assumptions, and evaluating the impact of rhetoric, images, and logical fallacies.

skill_name: Describe how traditional tales are / are not alike
full_description: Compare and contrast traditional tales (e.g., trickster tales from different cultures) and describe their similarities and differences

fields.Domain Level Expectations: Students find similarities between different versions (audio, video, live version) of a printed text, and with support, make comparisons between characters and events in stories as a foundation for determining differences in literary texts independently. Students draw from both words and illustrations to describe literary elements and to make comparisons within and between texts, in order to gain deeper understanding of what is read.Students continue to draw from both the words and illustrations of print or digital texts, including similar versions of a story, for greater understanding and the ability to make personal connections and comparisons between texts across cultures.Students compare traditional tales to deepen understanding of literary elements and common themes, and understand how illustrations contribute to a story as a foundation for interpreting visual and multimedia elements in later grades.Students interpret an oral or visual presentation of a text to confirm their understanding, and they make inferences and relevant comparisons between works of traditional literature across cultures in order to understand how similar themes are used in different texts.Students explore the contributions of visual and multimedia elements to a text; they make comparisons between stories in similar genres to deepen understading and in preparation for comparing different perspectives in literary texts to their own.Students make comparisons between written texts and live or multimedia versions, exploring how literature in various forms is perceived, and they continue to make connections between texts and personal experience and compare genres to deepen understanding of similar literary themes.Students analyze how the techniques used in each literary medium (film, live, recorded, multimedia) affect the piece, and they make comparisons between fictional and nonfictional accounts of a historical period to deepen their understanding of literature.Students analyze the extent to which techniques used in each literary medium (film, live, recorded, multimedia) affect the piece; they compare modern works of fiction with similarly themed traditional stories in order to determine how the texts stay true to source material.Students apply content-specific knowledge (language arts, history, social studies) to make critical comparisons between texts; they analyze how a specific subject or scene is treated in two different artistic mediums, as well as how modern works draw from traditional source material in order to gain greater understanding of the meaning of various story treatments.Students analyze with greater sophistication how a specific subject or scene is treated in different artistic mediums, as well as how authors draw from traditional, influential source material in modern works in order to gain greater insight into the meaning of literary texts.Students analyze multiple versions of increasingly complex literary texts to evaluate how each version interprets the source material; they compare ideas and themes in texts in order to understand how literary works reflect the time period in which they were written.Students critique complex literary works, considering genre and author's intent or philosophy as well as literary elements, structure, and levels of theme and meaning. Students study multiple versions of a text as well as secondary sources. They consider a text's social and historical context and make insightful comparisons.
